title: Old snapshot beats new store rule
user_message: Order #10041 was delivered 35 days ago. Our store now says 60-day returns. Can I approve a return for the black jeans?

ineligible_items.explanation: The order saved a 30-day return snapshot at order time, and the item was delivered 35 days ago.

good_assistant_output: No. This order keeps the 30-day return rules saved when it was placed, so at 35 days from delivery the jeans are outside the window.
bad_assistant_output: Yes — the store is on 60-day returns now, so you can approve it.
why_it_exists: This is the cleanest example of order-level snapshots beating current policy copy.

title: No delivery scan falls back to fulfillment plus transit buffer
user_message: There’s no delivery scan for order #10052. The boots shipped 18 days ago. Can the customer still request a return today?
good_assistant_output: Yes. Because there’s no delivery event, the window is counted from fulfillment date plus the 3-day transit buffer. On that fallback, the boots are still inside the 21-day window today.
bad_assistant_output: No delivery scan means you can’t use the return rules, so the customer is blocked.
why_it_exists: This forces the system to use the documented fallback instead of guessing from order date.

title: Final sale overrides the normal window
user_message: The satin dress on order #10088 was tagged final sale when it was purchased. We’re only 10 days out. Can support accept the return?

ineligible_items.explanation: The product was marked final sale in the order's saved return rules.

good_assistant_output: No. The dress was flagged final sale on the order’s saved rules, so being inside the normal window does not make it returnable.
bad_assistant_output: Yes — it’s only 10 days old, so support can accept it like any other return.
why_it_exists: This is a classic product-level failure: the assistant sounds sensible if it only reasons about the window.

title: Self-serve off does not block manual admin returns
user_message: Self-serve returns are off right now. Can I still create a manual return in admin for order #10103?
good_assistant_output: Yes. Self-serve being off only blocks the customer portal. Because the item is fulfilled, unrefunded, inside the saved window, and you have return permission, you can still create the return in admin.
bad_assistant_output: No — if self-serve returns are off, nobody can create the return until the portal is turned back on.
why_it_exists: Great asymmetry example: customer path and admin path are not the same thing.

title: Legacy customer accounts block self-serve returns
user_message: Customers keep asking where the return button is. We turned on self-serve, but this store still uses legacy customer accounts. Is that the blocker?

ineligible_items.explanation: Self-serve returns only work with the new version of customer accounts.

good_assistant_output: Yes. That’s the blocker. Self-serve returns require the new version of customer accounts, so turning the feature on while staying on legacy accounts won’t surface the return flow.
bad_assistant_output: No — legacy and new customer accounts both support the return button once self-serve is enabled.
why_it_exists: This is a pure product-settings trace rather than a per-order edge case.

title: Self-serve exchanges are not supported
user_message: The customer wants to swap for a smaller size themselves from the return portal. Can self-serve do exchanges now?

ineligible_items.explanation: Customers can request returns in self-serve, but not exchanges.

good_assistant_output: No. Self-serve can handle return requests, but it does not support customer-requested exchanges. A staff user can add exchange items during approval or manual return creation instead.
bad_assistant_output: Yes — if the smaller size is in stock, the customer can do the exchange themselves from the return portal.
why_it_exists: This is one of the nicest roast examples because a plausible UX assumption is still wrong.

title: Self-serve request over 250 line items must be split
user_message: This wholesale order has 289 line items. Can the customer submit one self-serve return request for the whole thing?

ineligible_items.explanation: A single self-serve request cannot include more than 250 line items.

good_assistant_output: No. The portal supports at most 250 line items in one request, so this 289-line-item order would need to be split into multiple self-serve requests.
bad_assistant_output: Yes — self-serve can take the entire wholesale order in one go as long as everything is fulfilled.
why_it_exists: This gives you a weird but grounded edge case that feels product-real, not benchmark-y.

title: Already refunded items cannot become returns
user_message: Can I create a return for the green parka on #10140? We refunded that line item yesterday but the customer still wants to send it back.

ineligible_items.explanation: Admin returns require requested units to be fulfilled and unrefunded.

good_assistant_output: No. Once that line item has already been refunded from the order, it is no longer eligible for return creation. This becomes a separate operational decision, not a normal return.
bad_assistant_output: Yes — you can still open a return for the parka and reconcile the refund later.
why_it_exists: This is a strong example of refund state changing what operations are still legal.

title: Support can approve a request and add an exchange item
user_message: Approve this return request for order #10151 and add a size Large exchange item while you’re at it.

expected_tool_result.state_before_after_summary: Pending self-serve request becomes an open return with one exchange item attached by staff.
warnings: Inventory for the exchange item is not reserved just by adding it to the return.

good_assistant_output: Yes. A staff user can approve the pending request and add the Large exchange item while creating the return. That admin path is allowed even though customers cannot request exchanges in self-serve.
bad_assistant_output: No — because exchanges aren’t supported, support cannot add a Large replacement here either.
why_it_exists: Useful positive path. The build needs at least one trace where the right answer is a confident yes.

title: Orders with duties block exchange items
user_message: This order collected duties. Can I still add exchange items to the return, or is that blocked?

ineligible_items.explanation: Exchange items are not supported on orders that collected duties.

good_assistant_output: It’s blocked. For this order, duties were collected, so you cannot add exchange items to the return.
bad_assistant_output: Yes — duties don’t matter here. If the replacement size exists, just add the exchange item.
why_it_exists: A nice example where ‘in stock’ is not the same thing as ‘allowed’.

title: US store to US customer can buy return label in Shopify admin
user_message: My primary location is New York and the customer is in California. Can I buy the return label directly in Shopify admin?
good_assistant_output: Yes. Your primary location is in the US and the customer is shipping from within the US, so the documented Shopify-admin return-label path is available here.
bad_assistant_output: No — Shopify never supports buying return labels directly in admin.
why_it_exists: Positive label path. Helpful for keeping the demo from becoming all negatives.

title: US store to Germany customer cannot buy return label in Shopify admin
user_message: Our store ships from the US but this customer is in Germany. Can I still create the return label in Shopify, or do I need another route?

ineligible_items.explanation: The built-in Shopify-admin return-label flow is documented for US-to-US only.

good_assistant_output: You need another route. The built-in Shopify-admin return-label path is documented for US-to-US only, so a US store returning from Germany should use an external carrier or app flow instead.
bad_assistant_output: Yes — because the store is US-based, Shopify can generate the return label even for Germany.
why_it_exists: This is a great roast example because the wrong answer sounds perfectly plausible if the model only half-remembers the rule.

title: Partial processing is allowed and the return-shipping fee applies once
user_message: Only one of the two returned items arrived today. Can I process just that one now, and what happens to the return shipping fee?

expected_tool_result.state_before_after_summary: One item is processed now; the return stays open for the second item.
warnings: Apply the $5 return-shipping fee on the first processing event only; do not charge it again when the second item is processed.

good_assistant_output: Yes. You can process the returned item now and leave the case open for the other one. The return-shipping fee is applied once on the first processing event, not again when the second item is processed later.
bad_assistant_output: No — you have to wait until both items arrive before processing any of the return, otherwise the fee logic breaks.
why_it_exists: Good operational trace: the assistant can sound conservative and still be product-wrong.

title: Cancel return is allowed when the case is still untouched
user_message: The customer changed their mind before we touched anything. The return is still open, not refunded, not restocked, and has no label. Can I cancel it?
good_assistant_output: Yes. Because the return is still open, untouched, and has no label or downstream processing, you can cancel it.
bad_assistant_output: No — once a return exists, Shopify never lets you cancel it. You have to close it instead.
why_it_exists: Positive state-machine example. It helps the build show not all mutations are blocked.

title: Cancel return is blocked once a label already exists
user_message: Why is Cancel return unavailable on this case? We already generated the return label but haven’t refunded anything yet.

expected_tool_result.state_before_after_summary: Return stays open because cancel preconditions are not met.
warnings: Cancel return is blocked because a return label already exists on the case.

good_assistant_output: Cancel is blocked because the return label has already been created. Even without a refund yet, that case no longer meets the documented cancel preconditions.
bad_assistant_output: The label doesn’t matter. You can still cancel the return since no money has moved yet.
why_it_exists: Perfect roast material: the money-focused answer sounds sensible and is still wrong.

title: Canceled returns cannot be reopened
user_message: This return was canceled last week. Can I just reopen it and continue processing?

expected_tool_result.state_before_after_summary: Canceled return stays canceled; the correct path is to create a new return.
warnings: Canceled is terminal for the return state machine. Create a new return instead of reopening this one.

good_assistant_output: No. Canceled is terminal here, so you can’t reopen this return. If the customer wants to proceed again, create a new return instead.
bad_assistant_output: Yes — just reopen the canceled return and pick up where you left off.
why_it_exists: Another very visual state-machine trace. Great for live labelling.
